speaker: interviewee
text: We arrived in Flossenburg. When we came to the camp, I saw a big sign, [SPEAKING GERMAN], I thought that we-- we are going to be treated like war prisoners. It won't be so bad. When they opened the gates, a [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] German officer, the Lagerkommandant, I think he was, the Hungarians reported that they brought 800 Jews and they-- they started counting. You know, the merchandise has to-- has to check out. They would count-- you know, that's why they put us in fours-- and they would count how many people we are. We got in. We got thrown into a-- to a big room, a big hall. We came into this big hall and-- and we saw-- we started seeing people that were dressed in those striped uniforms-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] this is vertical stripes. And on the back of their-- of their jackets was a-- a K slash L. So then we realized that the KL in German means konzentrationslager. This is not a prisoner of war camp, but it was a concentration camp. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] The-- the-- the-- The people in-- in that-- in that had-- you couldn't-- you couldn't see a normal look on their face. It-- it was just like-- like in-- in a crazy house. You-- you could see some of these people, they had some haircuts that was going like this, just cut through, and some of them, just the other-- the other way around. And some of-- some of them-- some, you heard all kinds of languages besides German. There was all kinds of languages-- Polish, Greek, Italian, all kinds of-- And-- and the people were mostly you could see just holes, the eyes sticking out. It was a terrible, terrible first impression, a sight that we saw. And then we all of a sudden saw through the window that a-- a group of women passed by in those Haftling pants and Haftling clothing jackets, but shaven off. So we could-- we could see that those were women, so we were sure now that this is not a-- a prisoner of war camp. It's a-- [SIGH]

speaker: interviewer
text: After-- after being in there, we tried to ask the people what-- what's going on? What is it here? The only answer we were getting is you-- you will find out. You will find out. They asked us, only thing they wanted, if we have cigarettes, you know? Because we were still in-- some people had still some cigarettes. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: A few hours later, we were in that-- in that room. They send in about 10 or 12 [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] so-called barbers and they would shave us all-- all off. But not-- not those crazy-- regular. Yeah, we were shaved off. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And from there-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] from there, we were sent to a-- to another-- to another big hall. I have to say, it was very cold and windy on that mountain. And in that other big hall, they ordered us to get undressed. So everybody used to get undressed and put together the way we did-- our shoes and our clothing, but here, they started the screams and the yellings and the beatings-- such barbaric and brutal screams. They wanted to dehumanize the people right away. You could-- you could see that. They didn't let you put-- put together your clothing. [SPEAKING GERMAN]. And if you started, getting beaten up like that. So we threw our clothing off. I remember, I had-- I had a-- a silver, very expensive watch that-- that my father had and this was-- I carried it with me and it was very sentimental for me. I-- I left it there. A cousin of mine in North [INAUDIBLE] gave me a-- a-- a necklace chain, a silver chain. I forgot it. Those that were-- were naked were ordered to go into another room and to get out the room through a window, not a door. The window was not too high, but you had to get out completely naked. And the reason for that was that they wanted to make sure that nobody has anything hidden in the most intimate places. I forgot to take my-- my silver-- silver chain off. It was ripped off, ripped off the chain, and got hit over the head on top of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when everybody got out through that window, we had to run for about 100 meters to a so-called entwesungsanlage. Entwesungsanlage is what the Germans called to-- to delouse yourself in a shower. We didn't know, at that time, that those euphemisms were used for putting gas instead of shower. But evidently, we were still 800 boys between the ages 20 to 40, in fairly good shape, and they could still use some-- some labor, some slave labor. So we didn't get gassed, but we got showered.

speaker: interviewee
text: We got showered and then they would, after the shower, they would throw to you a pair of pants, a pair of shoes, a shirt, and a jacket. No-- no-- never mind if you are six foot tall or you are five foot tall or if you weigh 150 pounds or you weigh 75 pounds. And we had-- we had to run with these. We came into our block where we were supposed to stay-- block number 20 in Flossenburg. And over there, we started to-- a small guy had a big pair of shoes, a big guy had a small pair of shoes, a short guy had long pants, a long guy had short pants, and we had to try to, among ourselves, to even it out, to exchange it. I was lucky that I was able to get a pair of shoes that I could put on and-- and-- and-- and-- and some-- and some clothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was not military shoes. Those-- those were shoes. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I remember, I think I-- I had-- I think those were shoes that they gave from people that-- that they-- they-- they gassed, that was leftover shoes. So it was all kinds. If you're lucky, I think I had a pair of-- a pair of like [SPEAKING GERMAN], you know, they were high shoes I was able to get. I didn't get it. I exchanged it because they were big and for whoever got it, I had a smaller one and we-- we exchanged-- exchanged it. And so we got into this-- this-- this camp and this was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] December the 23rd, 1944. And when we got into the camp-- I forgot to tell-- to tell you that in-- in the-- in this camp, they had three-- three different-- the uniforms had-- had triangles, those Haftling. Some triangles were green ones, some were red ones, and some were yellow ones. The-- the-- the red ones were murderers, German murderers that were sentenced to concentration camp. They were carrying the red triangles. And green ones were political prisoners. And the yellow ones were for the Jews. So we-- we-- we got all this-- this yellow-- yellow triangle clothing. Later on, they stamped the numbers on them.

speaker: interviewee
text: My number was 40,688. In-- in-- in that time, in December of 1944, don't forget, it was four months before the end of the war. They didn't-- they didn't tattoo anymore. Before, they were tattooing the people with the numbers, but it was too late for them. It-- it-- it-- they didn't have the time. I don't have any tattoos. But the number was 40,000. I still remember it. How much time do we have there? [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. They asked the-- the-- the-- the block-- the block-- each-- each area, each block had a [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. He happened to be one, a German, that had a-- had a red-- a red triangle-- he was some kind of a murderer. And-- and he-- he asked, who speaks more than one language? Who understands German, German, Hungarian? One of-- one of the doctors from out-- from our [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] said that he will translate. And he started giving out-- he started giving out the orders. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: What the rules are. What the slightest, slightest, digression is-- is death penalty. And he said, you are very lucky people because you came here and tomorrow, tomorrow is going to be Christmas and you are going to get a good meal. He said macaroni with marmalade, we are going to get tomorrow. And in the room, in this block, was already a Christmas tree was there. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what time we have to get up. And-- and-- and by that time, we were given-- we were given aluminum-- aluminum-- it's [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] what they had. You could use it for coffee. You could use it for-- for-- for soup. It had a-- a holder, that what we were given out. And that was our first day in-- in concentration camp. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Next day-- next day, we that-- that-- we that came, the new ones, were up to get our coffee. Breakfast was-- that wasn't coffee. It was ersatz cafe. You know, it was black, but it was hot. But we were not to go out. We were not to go out to work. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But the old ones that were in this-- in this block, they would go to their regular. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they went out to work and we-- a group came in and started registering everyone and asking them-- asking everyone if they had some kind of a trade. And-- and I remember that I-- I registered that I'm-- I'm a carpenter. I understand carpentry because I figured this is close. My parents were in the lumber business and it might be better than saying that you have no trade, whatsoever. Anyway, and then we went-- we went through that selection. The selection was a doctor that was standing on a-- on a pedestal and he was just piercing everybody with his eyes, looking over. Among our groups, I know-- he-- he and one guy would-- would mark-- mark, like they mark the cattle in-- in Texas, I saw on CNET with-- with-- with green or red paint on the forehead. They were-- a red one was-- was to death and a green one was to life. But most of our people that came that day were-- were passed-- passed through-- through for life. And I know one had a hernia and one guy, he was a married guy that had a-- he had a stomach operation done because he had a big-- big cut on the stomach. You could see it. He was taken out and we never-- we never saw him again. But here is the biggest spectacle that we encountered was Christmas Eve, 1944. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Next day, all the-- the labor units that went out to work were called in early and instead of taking them inside, into the block, where-- in-- in-- where they brought in the-- the food, whatever they-- whatever they had-- well, it was supposed to be the macaroni and-- and marmalade. They told them to-- to line up on the appelplatz. Lined up on the appelplatz, and then we got an order to-- to get out also. And this was like an amphitheater, like on a-- on a mountain. So we were-- we were, more or less, at the end-- at the end of-- of the group, and there were roughly between 5,000 and 6,000 inmates over there. We could-- we could see the-- the-- the-- the guards on the towers. They were all in the- in-- in-- in-- in the heavy pelts, clothing because it was very windy and very cold in December in Bavaria. It's-- it's-- we were shivering. We were standing there for about two hours. We couldn't-- you couldn't stand because these-- these Haftling clothings, those were linen, thin linen. It was-- I think this killed more people than the hunger because they were not from the cold. And then all of a sudden, we noticed-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] all of a sudden, we noticed that in-- in front-- in front, by the entrance, was a-- a big Christmas tree, illuminated at the entrance. And on the left side, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] an officer came in with four soldiers, walked out of the car, and here, all of a sudden, we noticed that there is a gallow, erected right at the front. From-- and-- and-- and it got dark already and it was all-- all illuminated. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the-- the officers with the four soldiers that came in. They called out three numbers, the three numbers-- each one had-- they knew-- knew their number. The three people and on the loudspeaker, they would read the-- the Haftling number, the Haftling name, and on the name of the Reichsfuhrer, Heinrich Himmler-- Himmler was from the-- from the SS. Those Haftling number so-and-so is sentenced to death by hanging for trying to escape. Another one, that was-- another-- that was a Russian soldier, a Russian officer. Another-- the other two were sentenced to hanging by-- by hitting back a Kapo-- that they were hitting back a Kapo. And after each one was taken over, brought over-- hanged, the-- the poor guys, the poor guys. I remember, we were no more cold when we saw that, what's happening. We were frozen numb. But they-- they yelled out, Hitler, Germany will pay for this! Comrades don't-- don't-- don't worry, Germany will pay for this crime. And that's how they-- they went to the gallows. And-- and after that spectacle, they dismissed us and we-- we got-- we got to the-- to the barracks. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: How much time you have there? Seven minutes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, after we got into the barracks, half frozen, like icicles, the-- the-- the-- the-- the wagons with the marmalade and the-- and the-- was-- was ready there and they were giving out the-- the holiday-- holiday meal. Of course, those of us that came in, the new ones, we-- we didn't-- we were not hungry. We-- we-- we didn't want to eat and we didn't eat any food, and after what we saw what was going on. The old ones said, you'll be here for a while. You'll eat. You'll eat. You'll see. And-- and-- and that was how we greeted Christmas 1944, December the 25th. Next day, we didn't go out. We didn't-- nobody went out to work. But block number 20-- the blocks were put up this way-- block number 21 was a so-called, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. [SPEAKING GERMAN] in German meant that this is like a-- a hospital. This is for sick people. When they-- you-- you register sick, then they put you into the [SPEAKING GERMAN]. And it was fenced off, but not a high fence. We couldn't go. We couldn't go across. But we saw, in the morning-- they-- they were a little bit higher, higher than we were. The door would open and they would just throw bodies out. They would throw a body out. And-- and they were piled up-- piled up bodies that died over-- over the night. And the pile like-- like-- like firewood. And then later-- later on, a group of Haftlings would come and-- and they would load them up on-- on-- on-- on carts and take them away, take them away to the crematorium. They had a crematorium in-- in Flossenburg. They would-- they would get rid of them like that, burn them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I-- we saw that for quite a few days. Many times, I was wondering, maybe some of them-- maybe some of them weren't still dead. But in this freezing weather, without-- you know, they were just in the [SPEAKING GERMAN] they were only in shirts. So they threw them out with-- with a shirt like you throw out garbage. And that's-- and that's-- that's how cheap-- that's how cheap life-- life was. How much time do we have? Two minutes. We'll stop. I- I have no-- no [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] to talk about it anymore.
